title: Pollo al ajillo
subtitle: Spanish garlic-and-sherry chicken, braised until golden in a pan of softened garlic.
excerpt: Pollo al ajillo is the garlic chicken of Spanish taverna cooking, bone-in pieces browned in olive oil, braised with a generous quantity of garlic, dry sherry, and a splash of stock until the garlic turns sweet and the sauce reduces to a glossy coat. Forty minutes and one pan. Bread is mandatory for the sauce.
sourceNotes: A classic tapa and main course of Spanish home cooking, particularly associated with Castilian and Extremaduran cooking. The technique of braising chicken with garlic in olive oil and finishing with sherry or white wine is documented across twentieth-century Spanish culinary literature. The garlic quantity, far more than most other cuisines would use, is the defining feature.

content.text: is not a subtle dish. An entire head of garlic, peeled and sliced, goes into the pan with the chicken and cooks down until sweet and golden. The sherry goes in halfway through and reduces with the stock to a concentrated, savoury sauce that coats the chicken pieces. It is the kind of recipe that makes the kitchen smell extraordinary from the moment the garlic hits the oil.

Bone-in, skin-on chicken pieces are essential. The skin crisps in the initial browning and then softens again in the braise, contributing flavour and fat to the sauce. Skinless boneless breast is the wrong choice; the dish becomes dry and the sauce has nothing to reduce into. Thighs and drumsticks are the most forgiving; a half chicken jointed into eight gives a more varied result for a dinner-party portion.

Dry sherry is the traditional liquid, fino or manzanilla are the right styles, dry and saline. A dry white wine works if you don't have sherry but the result is softer and less distinctive. The garlic quantity looks alarming but the long cooking turns it sweet and mild; the finished dish tastes of caramelised garlic rather than raw.

Season the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs generously with salt and pepper. Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a wide, heavy pan over medium-high heat until shimmering. Working in two batches if necessary, place the chicken skin-side down and cook for seven to eight minutes without moving until the skin is deep golden. Turn and cook for another three minutes on the flesh side. Transfer to a plate.

Reduce the heat to medium-low. Add {{garlic}} of sliced garlic to the oil remaining in the pan along with {{thyme}} of thyme and {{bay-leaves}} of bay. Cook gently for three to four minutes, stirring frequently, until the garlic is pale golden. Watch it, garlic that browns too fast turns bitter.

Pour in {{sherry-dry}} of dry sherry and let it bubble for thirty seconds, then add {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock. Return the chicken pieces to the pan, skin-side up. Bring to a gentle simmer, cover with a lid slightly ajar, and cook for twenty-five minutes until the chicken is cooked through. Remove the lid for the final five minutes to reduce the sauce.
Taste the sauce and adjust the salt. Scatter the chopped parsley over the top and bring the pan to the table. Crusty bread is not optional, the sauce is the point.

items.fix: Discard and start the garlic stage again with fresh cloves over low heat. The garlic needs gentle cooking for three to four minutes.
items.cause: Pan temperature too high when the garlic went in.
items.symptom: The garlic has burned and is bitter

items.fix: Remove the chicken and boil the sauce over high heat for three to four minutes until syrupy, then return the chicken.
items.cause: The lid was left on for the full cooking time.

items.fix: Pat the chicken skin dry before seasoning. Brown in one layer; crowding causes steaming rather than browning.
items.cause: The pan was not hot enough at the browning stage, or too many pieces were added at once.

content.text: Pollo al ajillo belongs to the tradition of Spanish cooking that uses the pan drippings as the sauce rather than building a separate one. It appears across Castile, Extremadura, and Andalusia as both a tapa (a smaller portion served with bread) and a main course. The same al ajillo technique is applied to prawns, rabbit, and mushrooms across Spanish regional cooking. The use of sherry rather than wine roots the dish firmly in the south; cooks in other regions might substitute a dry white Rueda or Albariño.
